verses.text: When a man hath taken a wife and she find no favour in his eyes because of some uncleanness, he shall write her a bill of divorcement, put it in her hand, send her out. If she become another man's wife, and the latter hate her or die, her former husband may not take her again to be his wife; she is defiled. Guidance: God did not invent divorce; He regulated it. The bill of divorcement gave the woman legal proof she was free to remarry. The 'no taking back' clause stopped men from passing wives between marriages like trade goods. Jesus would later note: Moses permitted it for the hardness of your heart. The text concedes the brokenness without endorsing it.

When a man hath taken a new wife, he shall not go out to war, neither be charged with any business: free at home one year, cheer up his wife which he hath taken. Guidance: One year off — no army, no business trips, no commission. The verb is precise: 'cheer up his wife.' The first year of marriage is given the protection of leisure. God writes joy into the calendar of new love. The kingdom does not consume the honeymoon for the harvest.

No man shall take the nether or upper millstone to pledge: for he taketh a man's life to pledge. Guidance: The millstone ground the family's daily bread. To seize it as loan security was to starve a household. God forbids any pledge that endangers life. The lender's right to security ends where the borrower's survival begins.

If a man be found stealing any of his brethren of the children of Israel, maketh merchandise of him, or selleth him; that thief shall die. So shalt thou put evil away from among you. Guidance: Human trafficking — the kidnap-and-sell pipeline — was a capital crime. God's law does not give the trader an inch. A person is not goods, and to handle a person as goods is to forfeit your own life. The strongest line drawn against the slave trade in any ancient code.

Take heed in the plague of leprosy, observe diligently as the priests the Levites teach you; remember what the LORD thy God did unto Miriam by the way, after ye were come out of Egypt. Guidance: The priestly diagnostics matter. And the example given is Miriam — the prophetess struck with leprosy for criticizing Moses. Even the leader's sister was not exempt. The chapter that protects widows and wives also remembers the day a beloved older sister sat outside the camp. Holiness is no respecter of persons, but God's mercy heals.

When thou dost lend thy brother any thing, thou shalt not go into his house to fetch his pledge. Stand abroad, the borrower shall bring out the pledge. If he be poor, sleep not with his pledge; in any case deliver it again at sundown, that he may sleep in his own raiment, and bless thee: it shall be righteousness unto thee. Guidance: Even the legal collection of debt has manners. Don't barge into a man's home; let him bring the security out. If the security is his coat, give it back before night so he doesn't freeze. The poor man's blessing is your righteousness — God registers it.

Thou shalt not oppress an hired servant that is poor and needy, of thy brethren or of strangers in thy land within thy gates. At his day give him hire, neither shall the sun go down upon it; for he is poor, setteth his heart upon it: lest he cry against thee unto the LORD, it be sin unto thee. Guidance: Pay your worker before sunset on the day he worked. He is feeding his family with that wage. If you delay, you wound; if you wound, he cries to God, and God hears. Withholding wages is a sin God personally answers.

The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, neither shall the children be put to death for the fathers: every man shall be put to death for his own sin. Guidance: Individual accountability. The clan-vengeance of the ancient world is refused. You answer for what you did, not for what your father did. This is the seed of justice that Ezekiel would later quote when reframing exile theology.

Thou shalt not pervert the judgment of the stranger, nor of the fatherless; nor take a widow's raiment to pledge. But thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in Egypt, and the LORD thy God redeemed thee thence: therefore I command thee this thing. Guidance: Three vulnerable categories named again — stranger, orphan, widow. The court must not bend against them. The widow's only coat must not be seized. And the reason given is memory: you were a slave; act like someone who remembers.

Thou shalt not pervert the judgment of the stranger, nor of the fatherless; nor take a widow's raiment to pledge. But thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in Egypt, and the LORD thy God redeemed thee thence: therefore I command thee this thing. Guidance: Three vulnerable categories named again — stranger, orphan, widow. The court must not bend against them. The widow's only coat must not be seized. And the reason given is memory: you were a slave; act like someone who remembers.

When thou cuttest down thine harvest, and hast forgot a sheaf in the field, thou shalt not go again to fetch it: it shall be for the stranger, the fatherless, the widow. When thou beatest thine olive tree, go not over the boughs again. When thou gatherest grapes, glean not afterward. Remember thou wast a bondman in Egypt. Guidance: A forgotten sheaf is not your harvest. The olive tree gets shaken once; the vine gets picked once; whatever remains belongs to the poor. God designs HARVEST itself to feed the hungry — the forgetfulness of the worker becomes the dinner of the widow. This is how Ruth fed Naomi.
